original_abstract: This book reflects and analyses the working of power in the field of global health– and what this goes on to produce. In so doing, Rethinking Global Health asks the pivotal questions of, ‘who is global health for’ and ‘what is it that limits our ability to build responses that meet people where they are?’ Covering a wide range of topics from global mental health to Ebola, this book combines power analyses with interviews and personal reflections spanning the author’s decade-long career in global health. It interrogates how the search for global solutions can often end up far from where we anticipated. It also introduces readers to different frameworks for power analyses in the field, including an adaptation of the ‘matrix of domination’ for global health practice. Through this work, Dr Burgess develops a new model of Transformative Global Health, a framework that calls researchers and practitioners to adopt new orienting principles, placing community interests and voices at the heart of global health planning and solutions at all times. This book will be beneficial to students and academics working in the global and public health landscape. It will also hold appeal to activists, practitioners and individuals invested in the discipline and in health equity around the world.
humanized_abstract: This book reveals and examines the exercise of power in the field of global health and its outcomes. As such, Rethinking Global Health examines some of the most critical issues for global health today – as fundamental as the questions of ‘who’ global health is ‘for’ and ‘what’ obstructs the creation of adaptive responses to the complex health needs of communities, populations and individuals worldwide. Spanning topics from global mental health to Ebola disease, this text powerfully brings together power analyses, interviews and personal anecdotes gathered by the author over the course of ten years of work in the field of global health. It provides perspectives on how the pursuit of global approaches to improving health can so often result in what is unexpected, unexpected in terms of not only the geographical locations that are involved, but also the broader social and health dynamics that emerge. Dr Burgess introduces a new paradigm for Global Health, Transformative Global Health, that points to a need for global health researchers and practitioners to reconsider the principles that guide and shape their practice; principles that would be anchored to considerations of the interests of communities and populations whose health is the subject of focus of global activity, and where the interests of those communities are taken into account at every stage and in all aspects of the global interventions and activities that are created to address their needs. This book is key reading for anyone involved in global and public health studies and will also be relevant to activists, practitioners and advocates working within the global health and health equity fields internationally.
